title: Grain boundary segregation of light elements and their effects on cohesion in ferritic steels
authors: Han Lin Mai, Xiang-Yuan Cui, Tilmann Hickel, Simon P. Ringer, Jörg Neugebauer
abstract: Light elements play an important role in influencing the macroscale properties of engineering alloys through grain boundary (GB) segregation phenomena. However, the scarcity and scattered nature of ab initio datasets for light elements in steels makes reproduction and extraction of general trends from the literature difficult. Here, we present a comprehensive ab initio evaluation of the segregation energies and cohesive effects for H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S, extensively sampling both substitutional and interstitial sites in six model coincident site lattice (CSL) ferritic iron GBs using density functional theory (DFT). Cohesive effects are evaluated in both a quantum-chemistry bond-order and rigid Rice-Wang interfacial cohesive strength framework. Our calculations indicate that, compared at the same concentration, B and C enhance GB cohesion, N, P, H are mildly detrimental, and He, O, S as powerful decohesive agents/embrittlers. Sampling both interstitial and substitutional starting positions is necessary to accurately capture segregation spectra. Commonly utilised sampling criteria such as site volumes prove insufficient for identifying deepest GB binding sites. Solutes placed in either kind of site can induce large relaxations to the same final configuration, resulting in site classification ambiguity. The nearest neighbour distance of a solute to its neighbours after relaxation is shown to be a controlling factor for the lower threshold of segregation energies at sites. The freely available DFT dataset and analysis repositories are expected to advance understanding of GB segregation behaviours of light elements in steels and serve as a resource for developing machine learning interatomic potentials.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive DFT-based dataset and analysis of how light elements segregate to grain boundaries in iron. It demonstrates that the ability of a site to relax and accommodate strain is a better predictor of segregation than initial void volume, and classifies elements as either strengtheners or embrittlers.
main_problem: Understanding how the segregation of light elements (H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S) to grain boundaries in ferritic steels affects interfacial cohesion and macroscale properties.
main_result: The study identifies B and C as cohesion enhancers, while He, O, and S are potent decohesion agents; it also reveals that segregation is driven by a site's ability to accommodate strain rather than initial Voronoi volume.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP package with the PBE functional, combined with bond-order (ANSBO) and Rice-Wang frameworks for cohesion analysis.
model_or_system: Six specific Coincident Site Lattice (CSL) grain boundaries in bcc-Fe (including Sigma3, Sigma5, Sigma9, and Sigma11) with various light element solutes in interstitial and substitutional sites.
key_observables: Segregation energies (Eseg), grain boundary energy (gamma_GB), work of separation (WRGS), and bond orders (ANSBO).
important_parameters: Solute types (H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S), site volumes, and nearest-neighbor distances after relaxation.
main_assumptions: The use of the PBE functional and the assumption that the Rice-Wang framework might be unphysical for certain elements like Helium due to rigid-grain separation assumptions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows GB atomic structures; Figure 3 shows the lack of correlation between GB energy and segregation strength; Figure 5 shows segregation energy distributions; Figure 7 shows the correlation between nearest-neighbor distance and segregation energy; Figures 8 and 9 show elemental cohesive effects.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of light element segregation, describes the DFT methodology and site sampling strategy, presents results on segregation energy trends and cohesion modifiers, analyzes the structural drivers of segregation (volume vs. strain), and concludes with data availability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The atomic structures of the six coincident-site-lattice model GBs investigated in this study, the (a) Σ3[110](1¯11), (b) Σ3[110](1¯12), (c) Σ5[001](210), (d) Σ5[001](310), (e) Σ9[110](2¯21) and the (f) Σ11[110](3¯32) GBs, respectively. The GB interface planes are highlighted by the dashed red lines. The shaded ranges indicate the range of studied sites for segregation, darker blue for interstitial sites, and the extended range in light blue for substitutional. The b-c projection (see Table 1) is presented in this Figure. The dimensions of these cells are given in Table 1. Structures were visualised using VESTA [48]

Figure 2: The starting positions considered for interstitial sites in this study for the (a) Σ3[110](1¯11), (b) Σ3[110](1¯12), (c) Σ5[001](210), (d) Σ5[001](310), (e) Σ9[110](2¯21) and (f) Σ11[110](3¯32) GBs, respectively.
Figure 3: The segregation energies at the strongest trapping sites are plotted against the GB energies of the pure Fe interfaces.

Figure 4: Comparison of the distance from the grain boundary versus segregation energy for solutes in the same grain boundary: (4a) H; (4b) He; (4c) B; (4d) C; (4e) N; (4f) O; (4g) P; (4h) S. Each panel shows the correlation between the distance of the solute’s most favorable site from the grain boundary plane and its minimum segregation energy, Eseg.

Figure 5: Distributions of segregation energies of the unique sites (after SOAP-based duplicate removal, Eseg < −0.1 eV) for each element after our duplicate removal procedure in our study: (5a) H; (5b) He; (5c) B; (5d) C; (5e) N; (5f) O; (5g) P; (5h) S. Bars are coloured by the starting site type: interstitial (blue), substitutional (orange), and mixed (green).
